speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two of an interview with Vera Ray. We were talking about running away with the refugees. Could you also talk a little bit about what had been before? What-- in Krakow, where did you live when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I remember a big house. I don't know whether it was a hotel, because it had a number of-- I think it was a hotel. It had many stories. And I can't-- the only thing that I can really remember of Krakow is the big bombardment. And I remember that very clearly. My father-- we always had trouble getting petrol. I mean, we had this car. And it just, it was an amazing car. It went on anything. My father got some blueberries one time, which had alcohol in it. The thing went on that. I mean, you know, somebody up there wanted us to keep moving, I reckon. And so he went off in search for, for petrol. And, and I do remember that bombardment. Because that was probably the biggest bombardment that I experienced. And I experienced a few. There were ambulances. There were sirens. There were bombs everywhere. And a bomb hit our building.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother basically panicked. She just-- she lost it, you know? And I remember standing in front of this house with fire, the whole thing just belching fire-- I think we had a suitcase-- and just standing. And my mother was just frozen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I never saw my mother cry before. And she was crying. And she had tears going down. And she's just standing there. And I remember the horror-- I was-- the, the horror of, of these ambulances, and the darkness, and the bodies, and the blood. And I kept calling out very dramatically-- I have often thought about it-- that I didn't know how to express this horror. I was saying, give me a knife. I want to kill myself. [LAUGHS] It's a bit embarrassing to actually-- it sounds so artificial. But I didn't know how else to say it. And my-- out of this smog, and fog, and mess, my father suddenly arrived with his little can of petrol. And he had been somewhere. And the bomb fell on the same building. And they ended up in the cellar. And there were thousands of people there. And they all panicked. And they couldn't get through the door, well, first of all, because  they panicked. Then everybody was trying to get out. And secondly, I think it-- the bomb fell on the building. And you know, it was-- you couldn't get the door open. But my father found another door. And he ended up in a shop that was sending-- selling Christian relics. And through this shop, he got out and, and came back with a little bit of dust on him. So we, honestly-- it was something humorous about a Jewish family being saved because there was a door to go into these Christian relics. I don't even know what to make of that. But I still think it's funny.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was-- that's my main memory of Krakow. I remember that I spoke Polish. We must have been there for a while, because I learned to speak Polish. I must have gone to school. I would have been seven, perhaps, by that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other thing I remember-- and again, the time, whether it's before or after-- it could have been after. Yes, after this bomb fell and we had to leave, we got into our car and hit the road again. And that was the time when the Polish army started running.

speaker: interviewee
text: All these beautiful officers with their wonderful sabers and horses couldn't really do much against tanks and things. And there was this river, river of, of people who wanted to escape. And there is a story to do with that. But I'll let you ask a question. [LAUGHS] Did you want to know more about Krakow? When you were in Krakow, were there other refugees like you yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were, there was-- there were some Jewish people. They must have been friends of ours or something. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Hm. I can remember a tall man who used to give me piggy backs. Doesn't help, does it? But I have a feeling that we must have had a bit of a group there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were the local people related to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: How were they relating to me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when, when the war broke out, you continued to, to move. And how did you decide where to go? How did your parents decide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just away from the Germans-- so I guess if you look at the map, they would be coming from the west. And we were moving east. The Germans were moving east. And we were moving east a few kilometers in front of them, hopefully.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were saying there were a lot of other people [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: The roads were chock-a-block full of people running with their little [INAUDIBLE], and bags, and kids. And the, the bombs were, were pretty scary. I remember. I mean, you know, you got so used to the bombs, you, you heard a siren if you were lucky. And you ran. But in the middle, I do remember in the middle of the road-- I don't know, somewhere through, it must have been through the countryside-- these planes came really, really low and just machine gun-- gunned the people that were walking. And so we had the routine of running and, and just jumping into a ditch. And I remember this one particular time, we really had to-- like, they were just chasing. They were just not, not very far at all, and my father grabbing me by the hand and pulling me. And I remember I could not keep up. My legs just wouldn't go as fast as his. And that was very weird, because I had to run. But I couldn't run that fast. And I think he half dragged me. He couldn't lift me. I mean, I was a big kid by the time I was seven or eight, maybe, seven-- so-- and then jumping into this, into this ditch. And you know, thank, thank goodness, we were safe again for a little minute. But-- for a little while until next time. And the, the-- this river of refugees-- and the Pole-- you know, the Polish army just gave in after a couple of days. And we were all fleeing. And everybody was going. And I remember at night, it was dark. I was presumably asleep in the back of the car. And I remember this gray mass of people. And the, the Polish army-- there was a little bridge. And we all wanted to cross. Because the-- because over the bridge was Russia. And-- but the Polish army wanted to get through that bridge, too. And my father revved up the car and just drove into the middle of this army. And the, the, the officer there was furious. But he wouldn't stop the whole army to get rid of one refugee. Because they weren't letting the refugees through. They wanted to save their own skin. So the refugees, the Jews, you know, and everybody else, they had to stay in the back. And I think they all perished, too. And that's how we got to Korets. And from Korets, I have a very good memory about everything. [LAUGHS] But up until Korets, it's very vague.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have-- in Poland, did you have any direct contact with the German army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope not. [LAUGHS] If they had contact with us, I probably wouldn't be here to tell the story. No, you see, this is what the bombardment was all about. That was when they-- I think they broke the treaty. Look, I'm not-- you know, I, I'm not big on the history-- historical facts. But from what I can gather, there was a treaty. The Germans broke it. They started to bombard Poland. And the Polish army gave up in a couple of days' time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was the beginning of the war. Now, which war? Was it '39 or was it '42? I don't know that the Russians were-- I think maybe the-- maybe it was '42. Couldn't have been. It must be '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: '39, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how old you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? How old?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how old you were then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wasn't eight. Because I didn't start school. Once we got into Russia, they don't take kids to school till their eight. And I, I didn't start school for a while. And then I started school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would be '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the incident of, of the train. How was it organized that the people went on the train? Was there any connection then with the Nazis, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: The train?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying that you were told that the men would be safe-- the, on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, where did this train come from? I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there wasn't any transport at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it must have been a trick. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was giving you this message?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did we know about this train? Poles, maybe the Poles-- it must have been the Poles. And what they-- where would they have taken these people in the train? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe at that time, the Poles and the Germans-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] look, I don't know. But my suspicion is that the Poles wanted to get rid of the Jews. And that was one way of doing it, get rid of the men, and-- you know, get the men to get on the train and take them someplace where they don't want to be. And then deal with the children and the women and, you know, clear the, clear the roads so that the soldiers can get through. But I was six, seven years old. I'm just-- you know, this is hindsight. So I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in Korets when you arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Korets was a nice Yiddish shtetl. But it wasn't so easy to actually just arrive, because we crossed this bridge. That was one of the few times where we were-- actually didn't have any food to eat. But there's always food except that time. I remember. I half  remember, half been told-- that, that I asked that I was hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, well, there isn't any food. And I said, well, when will there be food? And they said, we don't know. I remember that as very scary, that they couldn't say to me, well, wait till 3 o'clock, and you'll have your dinner then. It was like, we don't know. So the, the Russians stopped us and said we were spies. Why were we spies? Because we had a Czech passport. And we ended up in Russia. They could not understand that we actually didn't cross any borders, that the border came to us, you know? The, the, the Poles took us over. And then the Russians took over Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my father had all these clocks and watches from the, from the shops. So not only were we spies, we were capitalists. However, my father was a good watchmaker. And they needed watchmakers. And he was set up to teach everyone how to be a watchmaker.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we, so we stayed in this Korets. It was a, it was a shtetl. But my god, it was primitive. There was nothing. There was no electricity. There was no gas. There was no-- I remember it was very-- and I learned Yiddish. And suddenly, I went to a Yiddish school. And I was writing from right to left. And it was indescribably dirty, like there was nowhere-- the people thought that my mother was crazy because she insisted on washing me once a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember a little girl friend-- lice everywhere. And I said to her, apparently, where I come from, we don't have lice. And the next day, she came back and she said, my grandfather said that you're a liar. There is no such thing in the world where they don't have lice. So that was the end of that one. But, yeah, Korets was OK. But then, then the front started to move. And we started to move. But you know, there are a lot of things to say about Korets where the, the, the, you know, the communists didn't like the Jews, either. And we lived with a balabusta. She was the balabusta. She was the-- it was a-- it was like a-- well, it was  a house. And she had three children. And one, one, one day, in the middle of the night, the secret police knocked on the door. And they took away her eldest daughter, Golde.

speaker: interviewee
text: While you were there? No. That was just before we came. And she just went crazy. And every year since then, she would run around the house at the anniversary, and I think maybe every month. I don't know what the anniversaries were. And she would scream and, you know, [NON-ENGLISH] and pulling her hair and-- because Golde never came back. So yep, anti-Semitism in Russia was virulent, virulent. And I experienced it quite a bit. How was it expressed towards you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, in Korets, actually, it wasn't so bad. Because everybody was Jewish except for the higher ups. Some of the, the Russian, you know, the, the bosses. The bosses were all Russian with lovely, you know, children with fair hair and all this stuff. I must have gone to a Russian school-- see, I reckon that the first half of when we were in Korets, it was still Polish. Because that was a Yiddish school. And then I went to a Russian school. And I remember having to start writing the other end, and the other way, and a different alphabet. And that's where there was this lovely girl with, you know, blonde hair. And she had really nice clothes. And I was very jealous.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was-- and she had good paper. We wrote on, you know, bits of newspaper, on the edges of newspaper. And she had all this beautiful paper. Funny things kids remember, huh? So that was Korets. Did your parents explain to you what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was the worst thing, you know? That-- I really suffered from that, that I never was told why. I mean, I'm better off than a friend of mine who was told, you're a naughty girl. This is why this is happening to you. You know, they wanted to give an explanation. They stuck her in the cellar to hide her, and told her you have to go to the cellar because you're a naughty girl. So I think I was better off. At least I wasn't told anything. But I knew that there was something wrong with being Jewish. There was something bad about it. And it wasn't that I was a bad girl. It was-- there was something else. It was very mysterious and scary. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Korets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [LAUGHS] probably a year. But I think it was about a year, maybe a bit longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you have to leave? Or why did you move  on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were just constantly running away from the Germans. Next thing I knew, they bombarded Kiev. Oh, that's right. My mother was, she was retraining as a teacher. She went to Kiev to do some exams. No it wasn't Kiev. It was Lwow, Lwow. And I was the naughty girl. And I, I apparently threw a rock at somebody, and fell on top of this rock, and gashed my leg. And they were-- my mother was away for the first time in her life. And I was dying of this gangrene. And she had to come back. But within a few-- so she didn't get her qualifications. But, but it was very soon after that that they bombarded [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Kiev. And, and that's when-- I hope these dates are, are, are half reasonable. What I know for sure is we went to Kiev. We were running away from the Germans again. They put us in these cattle, cattle trains. And we went of somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who put you, who put you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians in Kiev. Yeah. And we ended up in Asia. We were, we're in these trains for forever, forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the people with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there? Was there any round up, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: On this train? I don't know. We didn't have a car by then. We've spent a lot of time on the railway stations. Because I remember we had this very narrow fold-up bed. And my mother and I slept toe and, you know, head, one head there. And my father was just sitting on this suitcase. He was sitting on a suitcase. There was a lot of trains and sitting and running and-- but I do remember this cattle train with the straw. Because I'd never slept on straw before. We ended up in a place called Kzyl-Orda near Alma-Ata. And that's Kazakhstan. Like, that's far away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you know how to spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. [LAUGHS] It's capital K-Z-Y-L dash capital O-R-D-A. But that was a small-- actually, I had a look on the map the other day. And it's actually on the map. But I remember it was a tiniest little place. And it was near a big place called Alma-Ata. That's A-L-M-A dash A-T-A. And I remember that we lived in a-- this earthen hut with an earthen floor with straw. And there was no running water or electricity. And my father was bitten by a camel. That was highly exciting, that he, he went to the market. And he was spat at by a camel. And he came home with his torn jacket. So you see, this is what kids remember-- nothing-- some, you know, sort of trivial stuff, like my dad was bitten by a camel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you told why they put you-- why they took you to, to this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any supervision? Did you have any supervision there? Or were you free?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were free. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, I talked-- the-- a friend of mine, whom I basically grew up with, Dorothy. She, she died of cancer a couple of years ago. But she's-- our fathers came from the same village. And I think we're distant related, actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: They ended up in Siberia. And we didn't. Why? Because they-- the parents had to fill in this form. And one of the questions was after the war, will you be returning to Czechoslovakia? And her father said, yes. And my father said, no. And that made the difference between, you know, a camp in Siberia and being relatively free in, in Asia. I think they needed a, a watchmaker in Asia. I don't know. So my father had a really strong sense of self-preservation. I mean, he had no intention of staying in Russia, for heaven's sakes. But he knew what to say. So he said--

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no. We, we will stay in Russia forever. Because we, we think it's a wonderful country. Yeah. And, and, and poor old Mr. Altman said, they'll tell the truth. Yes, I want to go home. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that-- well, I remember about anti-Semitism was about what my mother told me, which was that she was teaching school. And she had, she had terrible trouble from-- about anti-Semitism, you know, the-- oh, first of all, from the, from the adults, from the teachers, but not only from the teachers, but from the hierarchy that the communist-- you know, the people who-- I don't know what they were, secret police or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were actually constantly monitoring all the teachers. And I mean, my mother was supposed to teach English, and, and, and German, and French. But she had to teach the and all this sort of stuff. And there was a lot of anti-Semitism. I mean, I remember anti-Semitism a little bit later on, where the kids-- that was in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was in [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah. There were three of us in the, in the classroom-- three Jews. I was an, an underground Jew. I was very happy when they called me [? Cheska. ?] [? Cheska ?] [? chaska-- ?] I thought, oh, thank goodness, they're not calling me Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cheska means Czech. And chaska is nothing. It means like cup. It was-- the-- you know, kids are always teasing each other. But at least it wasn't called dirty Jew. But there were two other children in my-- in the classroom. And one boy was beaten to a pulp every day. He was not a very attractive child, I remember. And they beat him something terrible. And finally, the parents came to complain to the school. And the principal said, it's his own fault. He starts all the fights. Now, this boy was beaten to thread because he was a Jew. There was a girl who was also Jewish. They didn't beat her, because she was quiet. She was attractive. She was intelligent. And she kept their mouth shut.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they, they just hated that boy. He looked a little bit Jewish, you know, with a little bit of a nose and all this. It was terrible. They, they cut up his coat. And he, he walked in the, in the, you know, corridor where there were lot of kids. And the kids, the kids, the, the kids were, I mean, you know, and all this stuff they're complaining about. Now, that's nothing-- nothing. I mean, the kids didn't have guns at school. But, boy, it was-- and it was directed at Jews. It was directed at Jews. Because they thought that I was Czech, they didn't bother me too much. But sometimes they asked me, because with a name like Silbiger, you know? I said it's a Czech name.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- my parents taught me to hide. They did not teach me to stand up to, to this kind of bullying. And in a way, they did the right thing. Because I had no chance of winning. But it did not help me. And it took me years to get over this total paranoia of anti-Semitism. And then when I started to identify as a lesbian, I was also closeted. And, you know, fear was ruling my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, at  one stage, I decide I've had enough of this. This is who I am. Take it or leave it. But it took me many years to get through that enormous fear and this kind of, you know, fog about what is happening and why is it happening? What have I done wrong? Until I finally realized, I've done nothing wrong. And it's been better since then. [DRINKS] How old were your-- were the children then, the incidents that you're describing in the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Primary school-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I only went up to about-- nah, it couldn't have been third grade. Yeah,  it could have been-- eight, nine, 10. They were 10-year-old-- 10, 11 maybe. But it was bad, believe me. You have killed Christ. And you are drinking the blood of babies-- I mean, disgusting stuff, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you telling your parents about those incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because I know what my mother would have said. She would have said, well, don't tell them you're Jewish, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: So don't tell them. You don't have to tell them. Tell them, no, you're not Jewish. And my father-- I think by that stage, my father was, was fighting in the front, you know? And now, we've finished the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I thought we might. [LAUGHS]
